title: The Last Ride Together
content:
I.
آ آ آ آ I said, Then, dearest, since ï؟½tis so,
آ آ آ آ Since now at length my fate I know,
آ آ آ آ Since nothing all my love avails,
آ آ آ آ Since all, my life seemed meant for, fails,
آ آ آ آ Since this was written and needs must be
آ آ آ آ My whole heart rises up to bless
آ آ آ آ Your name in pride and thankfulness!
آ آ آ آ Take back the hope you gave, I claim
آ آ آ آ Only a memory of the same,
آ آ آ آ And this beside, if you will not blame,
آ آ آ آ Your leave for one more last ride with me.

II.
آ آ آ آ My mistress bent that brow of hers;
آ آ آ آ Those deep dark eyes where pride demurs
آ آ آ آ When pity would be softening through,
آ آ آ آ Fixed me, a breathing-while or two,
آ آ آ آ With life or death in the balance: right!
آ آ آ آ The blood replenished me again;
آ آ آ آ My last thought was at least not vain:
آ آ آ آ I and my mistress, side by side
آ آ آ آ Shall be together, breathe and ride,
آ آ آ آ So, one day more am I deified.
آ آ آ آ Who knows but the world may end tonight?

III.
آ آ آ آ Hush! if you saw some western cloud
آ آ آ آ All billowy-bosomed, over-bowed
آ آ آ آ By many benedictions sunï؟½s
آ آ آ آ And moonï؟½s and evening-starï؟½s at once
آ آ آ آ And so, you, looking and loving best,
آ آ آ آ Conscious grew, your passion drew
آ آ آ آ Cloud, sunset, moonrise, star-shine too,
آ آ آ آ Down on you, near and yet more near,
آ آ آ آ Till flesh must fade for heaven was here!
آ آ آ آ Thus leant she and lingered joy and fear!
آ آ آ آ Thus lay she a moment on my breast.

IV.
آ آ آ آ Then we began to ride. My soul
آ آ آ آ Smoothed itself out, a long-cramped scroll
آ آ آ آ Freshening and fluttering in the wind.
آ آ آ آ Past hopes already lay behind.
آ آ آ آ What need to strive with a life awry?
آ آ آ آ Had I said that, had I done this,
آ آ آ آ So might I gain, so might I miss.
آ آ آ آ Might she have loved me? just as well
آ آ آ آ She might have hated, who can tell!
آ آ آ آ Where had I been now if the worst befell?
آ آ آ آ And here we are riding, she and I.

V.
آ آ آ آ Fail I alone, in words and deeds?
آ آ آ آ Why, all men strive and who succeeds?
آ آ آ آ We rode; it seemed my spirit flew,
آ آ آ آ Saw other regions, cities new,
آ آ آ آ As the world rushed by on either side.
آ آ آ آ I thought, All labour, yet no less
آ آ آ آ Bear up beneath their unsuccess.
آ آ آ آ Look at the end of work, contrast
آ آ آ آ The petty done, the undone vast,
آ آ آ آ This present of theirs with the hopeful past!
آ آ آ آ I hoped she would love me; here we ride.

VI.
آ آ آ آ What hand and brain went ever paired?
آ آ آ آ What heart alike conceived and dared?
آ آ آ آ What act proved all its thought had been?
آ آ آ آ What will but felt the fleshly screen?
آ آ آ آ We ride and I see her bosom heave.
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s many a crown for who can reach,
آ آ آ آ Ten lines, a statesmanï؟½s life in each!
آ آ آ آ The flag stuck on a heap of bones,
آ آ آ آ A soldierï؟½s doing! what atones?
آ آ آ آ They scratch his name on the Abbey-stones.
آ آ آ آ My riding is better, by their leave.

VII.
آ آ آ آ What does it all mean, poet? Well,
آ آ آ آ Your brains beat into rhythm, you tell
آ آ آ آ What we felt only; you expressed
آ آ آ آ You hold things beautiful the best,
آ آ آ آ And pace them in rhyme so, side by side.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis something, nay ï؟½tis much: but then,
آ آ آ آ Have you yourself whatï؟½s best for men?
آ آ آ آ Are you poor, sick, old ere your time
آ آ آ آ Nearer one whit your own sublime
آ آ آ آ Than we who never have turned a rhyme?
آ آ آ آ Sing, ridingï؟½s a joy! For me, I ride.

VIII.
آ آ آ آ And you, great sculptor, so, you gave
آ آ آ آ A score of years to Art, her slave,
آ آ آ آ And thatï؟½s your Venus, whence we turn
آ آ آ آ To yonder girl that fords the burn!
آ آ آ آ You acquiesce, and shall I repine?
آ آ آ آ What, man of music, you grown grey
آ آ آ آ With notes and nothing else to say,
آ آ آ آ Is this your sole praise from a friend,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Greatly his operaï؟½s strains intend,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Put in music we know how fashions end!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ I gave my youth; but we ride, in fine.

IX.
آ آ آ آ Who knows whatï؟½s fit for us? Had fate
آ آ آ آ Proposed bliss here should sublimate
آ آ آ آ My being, had I signed the bond
آ آ آ آ Still one must lead some life beyond,
آ آ آ آ Have a bliss to die with, dim-descried.
آ آ آ آ This foot once planted on the goal,
آ آ آ آ This glory-garland round my soul,
آ آ آ آ Could I descry such? Try and test!
آ آ آ آ I sink back shuddering from the quest.
آ آ آ آ Earth being so good, would heaven seem best?
آ آ آ آ Now, heaven and she are beyond this ride.

X.
آ آ آ آ And yet, she has not spoke so long!
آ آ آ آ What if heaven be that, fair and strong
آ آ آ آ At lifeï؟½s best, with our eyes upturned
آ آ آ آ Whither lifeï؟½s flower is first discerned,
آ آ آ آ We, fixed so, ever should so abide?
آ آ آ آ What if we still ride on, we two
آ آ آ آ With life for ever old yet new,
آ آ آ آ Changed not in kind but in degree,
آ آ آ آ The instant made eternity,
آ آ آ آ And heaven just prove that I and she
آ آ آ آ Ride, ride together, for ever ride?
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
